title: About Iris KNN
tagline: How a 1936 dataset, K-Nearest Neighbors, and a 4-layer architecture come together on pandyaHomeLab.

sections.title: The Problem
body: The Iris dataset (Fisher, 1936) is one of the most famous benchmarks in machine learning. It contains 150 flowers across three species — *setosa*, *versicolor*, and *virginica* — each with four measurements: sepal length, sepal width, petal length, and petal width.

The classification problem: given those four measurements for an unseen flower, predict its species. It's small, clean, and beautifully balanced (50 samples per class), which makes it a perfect teaching example for supervised classification.

sections.title: Approach — Why KNN?
body: K-Nearest Neighbors is a non-parametric, instance-based algorithm. To classify a new flower, it:
1. Computes the Euclidean distance from the new sample to every training sample
3. Returns the majority species among those three

bullets.label: Why KNN here
bullets.text: 150 samples is small — KNN's O(n) lookup is trivial, and there's no overfitting from too few neighbors.

bullets.label: Why k=3
bullets.text: An odd k avoids ties; small k captures local structure since the species clusters are well-separated in petal-length × petal-width space.

bullets.label: Why no scaling
bullets.text: All four features are in centimeters — already on the same scale. No StandardScaler needed.

sections.title: Architecture
body: Every project on pandyaHomeLab follows the **per-project 4-layer architecture** (ADR-013) — each domain is autonomous, with no cross-project imports.

sections.title: Network & Deployment
body: The platform uses Docker bridge networks for domain isolation (ADR-016). The ML domain has its own subnet — services there can talk to each other directly, but external traffic must pass through the proxy network.

sections.title: Performance
body: Trained on a 80/20 split with `random_state=42`. Iris is famously easy — 100% test accuracy is achievable with most algorithms because the species are well-separated in petal feature space.
Because the dataset is **balanced** (50 samples per species), accuracy is an honest measure here. We use *macro* averaging for precision/recall/F1 so each species counts equally.

confusion_matrix.annotation: Petal length and width separate the three species cleanly. When the diagonal accounts for every test sample, KNN's nearest-neighbor lookup never crosses a class boundary on this split.

metrics_detail.name: Accuracy
metrics_detail.what: Fraction of test flowers classified correctly — the most intuitive measure of overall correctness.
metrics_detail.why_chosen: Iris is perfectly balanced (50/50/50). When classes are equal, accuracy is honest and easy to explain. On imbalanced data it can lie — a model that always predicts the majority class might score 95% while learning nothing.
metrics_detail.watch_out: Doesn't reveal *which* species the model confuses with which — for that, look at per-class precision and recall.

metrics_detail.name: Precision (macro)
metrics_detail.what: When the model predicts a species, how often is it right? Averaged equally across all three species.
metrics_detail.why_chosen: *Macro* averaging treats every species equally regardless of sample count — appropriate here since all three species have 50 samples each. Catches false positives, e.g. mislabelling a *versicolor* as *virginica*.
metrics_detail.watch_out: High precision alone can hide missed positives — always pair with recall.

metrics_detail.name: Recall (macro)
metrics_detail.what: Of the actual flowers of a species, what fraction did the model correctly find?
metrics_detail.why_chosen: Catches missed positives. If the model never predicted *virginica*, that class's recall would be 0 — even with otherwise high accuracy. Macro averaging ensures rare classes aren't drowned out.
metrics_detail.watch_out: High recall can come at the cost of false alarms — always pair with precision.

metrics_detail.name: F1 (macro)
metrics_detail.what: Harmonic mean of precision and recall — penalises models that only do one well.
metrics_detail.why_chosen: A single number that captures both error types. Because it's a *harmonic* mean (not arithmetic), F1 collapses if **either** P or R is poor. F1 = 1.0 truly means both are perfect, not just on-average decent.
metrics_detail.watch_out: Doesn't account for true negatives — it's a per-class measure, not a global correctness measure like accuracy.
